case.title: Batch Synthesis of 3,5-Disubstituted Isoxazole in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
case.protocol: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).
case.notes: Model-matrix benchmark for upstream/council combinations on the DES isoxazole protocol.

chemistry_plan.reaction_name: 1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition of phenylacetylene with ethyl nitroacetate to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate
chemistry_plan.bond_formed: C-O and C-C bonds forming the isoxazole ring
chemistry_plan.bond_broken: N-O bond rearrangement in nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate

reagents: ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv) in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
feed_streams.reasoning: Both substrates must be premixed in DES before entering the heated zone so the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide is immediately trapped by phenylacetylene, minimizing dimerization to furoxan. Single stream avoids mixing issues with viscous DES.

reagents.name: ethyl nitroacetate
reagents.notes: Generates nitrile oxide in situ at elevated temperature; 2 equiv used to drive conversion

reagents.name: tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB)
reagents.role: deep eutectic solvent component (hydrogen bond acceptor)
reagents.notes: Forms DES with ethylene glycol; acts as reaction medium promoting nitrile oxide generation

reagents.name: ethylene glycol (EG)
reagents.role: deep eutectic solvent component (hydrogen bond donor)

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal dehydration/tautomerization of ethyl nitroacetate to generate ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide (nitrile oxide intermediate) in situ, facilitated by the DES medium at elevated temperature
Concerted [3+2] 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the nitrile oxide (C≡N⁺-O⁻) across the C≡C triple bond of phenylacetylene, forming the isoxazole ring with regioselectivity placing phenyl at C-5
Tautomeric aromatization of the initial cycloadduct to give the fully aromatic isoxazole product

stream_logic.reasoning: Single premixed stream: both substrates dissolved in DES at room temperature before entering the heated reactor coil. Premixing ensures the nitrile oxide intermediate is immediately trapped by the dipolarophile upon formation, suppressing dimerization. DES viscosity (~30-50 cP at RT) favors a single-stream approach to avoid poor mixing of two viscous streams at a T-mixer.

chemistry_plan.mixing_order_reasoning: Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate are premixed in DES at room temperature where no reaction occurs. The mixture is then pumped into the heated coil reactor. This ensures homogeneous composition when the nitrile oxide forms at elevated temperature, maximizing trapping efficiency and minimizing furoxan byproduct from nitrile oxide dimerization.
chemistry_plan.deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species; thermal pericyclic mechanism is unaffected by O₂
chemistry_plan.quench_reasoning: Reaction reaches completion rapidly; cooling the outlet stream to room temperature is sufficient to stop any further reaction. Batch work-up (water quench, DCM extraction) is for isolation, not quenching a reactive intermediate.
chemistry_plan.wavelength_reasoning: No photochemistry involved; reaction is thermally driven.
chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism (well-established nitrile oxide [3+2] cycloaddition). The exact mechanism of nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate in DES (thermal dehydration vs. DES-mediated) is somewhat uncertain — literature suggests thermal elimination of water. Superheating to 140°C in flow is speculative but reasonable given the 15 min batch time at 120°C. DES viscosity at operating temperature is estimated; actual value should be measured for precise pressure drop and residence time calculations. The 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate accounts for competitive nitrile oxide dimerization; in flow with better mixing and temperature control, it may be possible to reduce to 1.5 equiv.

intensification_mandate.flow_justification_basis: Flow provides three key advantages: (1) Superheating under back-pressure regulation (e.g., 140°C, 5-10 bar BPR) accelerates nitrile oxide generation and cycloaddition, reducing residence time from 15 min to ~5 min. (2) Narrow-bore tubing (1.0 mm ID, stainless steel or PFA) provides excellent heat transfer (surface-to-volume ratio ~4000 m⁻¹) for rapid, uniform heating of the viscous DES, eliminating thermal gradients present in batch oil-bath heating. (3) Continuous operation with the DES enables potential solvent recycling and higher throughput without scale-up of the exothermic cycloaddition. Note: DES viscosity requires careful pressure drop calculation via Hagen-Poiseuille; a 1.0 mm ID × 2 m coil at 0.2 mL/min with ~20 cP viscosity (at 120°C) gives ΔP ≈ 3 bar, manageable with standard BPR.

final_design_candidate.human_explanation:
## Flow Chemistry Design for 1,3-Dipolar Cycloaddition

### (1) Proposed Flow Setup

**Single-stream thermal coil reactor:**
- **Residence time:** 2.8 min
- **Flow rate:** 3.8154 mL/min  
- **Reactor:** 10.683 mL stainless steel coil, 0.5 mm ID
- **Temperature:** 140.0 °C (superheated under pressure)
- **Back-pressure regulator:** 5.0 bar
- **Feed:** Single premixed stream containing phenylacetylene (1.0 equiv), ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv), and TBAB/EG (1:5) deep eutectic solvent at 0.5 M concentration

**Stream configuration:** All reagents premixed in DES at room temperature, then pumped through heated coil. No mixing required in the reactor since reaction only occurs at elevated temperature.

### (2) Rationale for Conditions

The design leverages **superheating under back-pressure** (140°C vs 120°C batch) to accelerate both nitrile oxide generation and cycloaddition kinetics, targeting ~3× residence time reduction from 15 min to 2.8 min. This approach parallels thermal intensification strategies used in similar pericyclic reactions.

The **0.5 mm ID tubing** provides excellent heat transfer (surface-to-volume ratio ~8000 m⁻¹) critical for rapid, uniform heating of the viscous DES medium. The **5.0 bar BPR** enables superheating above the solvent boiling point while maintaining liquid phase operation.

**Single-stream design** avoids mixing challenges with viscous DES (~30-50 cP) that would occur at T-mixers. Since no reaction occurs at room temperature, premixing is safe and ensures homogeneous composition when nitrile oxide forms at high temperature.

### (3) Key Differences from Batch Protocol

- **Temperature elevation:** 140°C vs 120°C batch for accelerated kinetics
- **Pressure operation:** 5.0 bar BPR vs atmospheric batch
- **Residence time:** 2.8 min vs 15 min batch (5.4× reduction)
- **Heat transfer:** Narrow-bore tubing provides rapid, uniform heating vs oil bath gradients
- **Continuous operation:** Enables potential DES recycling and higher throughput

### (4) Engineering Validation Warnings

⚠️ **Mass transfer limitations identified:** Damköhler number (Da = 39.4) indicates potential mixing limitations in laminar flow, though acceptable for single-stream design.

⚠️ **Viscosity sensitivity:** DES viscosity temperature dependence may affect pressure drop during startup vs steady-state operation. Monitor pressure during heat-up.

⚠️ **Flow enhancement uncertainty:** The estimated 12.5× intensification factor from superheating is literature-based and requires experimental verification for this specific DES system.

**Recommended validation:** Verify residence time and conversion at design conditions before scale-up. Consider tracer studies to confirm mixing performance.

**Confidence: LOW** — The closest literature analogy has a similarity score of 0.38 (threshold for MEDIUM: 0.50). No close precedent found in the corpus for this exact reaction class and conditions. Treat this proposal as a starting hypothesis requiring careful experimental validation before scale-up.

Candidate 4 wins with combined score 0.661, driven by exceptional productivity and geometry scores despite moderate domain performance. The 1.6 mm ID provides optimal balance of heat transfer (surface-to-volume ratio ~2500 m⁻¹) and low pressure drop (0.090 bar) for the viscous DES. Runner-up candidate 14 has identical productivity but longer residence time (2.0 vs 1.2 min), while candidate 9 has similar geometry but slightly lower conversion (0.96 vs 0.80). The balanced objective favors candidate 4's superior residence-time reduction meeting the 3× target. Key remaining uncertainty is whether the estimated 12.5× flow enhancement factor from superheating and improved heat transfer will be realized experimentally. The first bench experiment should verify residence time and conversion at the design conditions of 1.2 min, 140°C, 5 bar BPR with 1.6 mm ID tubing.

formatted_result.explanation:
## Flow Chemistry Design for 1,3-Dipolar Cycloaddition

### (1) Proposed Flow Setup

**Single-stream thermal coil reactor:**
- **Residence time:** 2.8 min
- **Flow rate:** 3.8154 mL/min  
- **Reactor:** 10.683 mL stainless steel coil, 0.5 mm ID
- **Temperature:** 140.0 °C (superheated under pressure)
- **Back-pressure regulator:** 5.0 bar
- **Feed:** Single premixed stream containing phenylacetylene (1.0 equiv), ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv), and TBAB/EG (1:5) deep eutectic solvent at 0.5 M concentration

**Stream configuration:** All reagents premixed in DES at room temperature, then pumped through heated coil. No mixing required in the reactor since reaction only occurs at elevated temperature.

### (2) Rationale for Conditions

The design leverages **superheating under back-pressure** (140°C vs 120°C batch) to accelerate both nitrile oxide generation and cycloaddition kinetics, targeting ~3× residence time reduction from 15 min to 2.8 min. This approach parallels thermal intensification strategies used in similar pericyclic reactions.

The **0.5 mm ID tubing** provides excellent heat transfer (surface-to-volume ratio ~8000 m⁻¹) critical for rapid, uniform heating of the viscous DES medium. The **5.0 bar BPR** enables superheating above the solvent boiling point while maintaining liquid phase operation.

**Single-stream design** avoids mixing challenges with viscous DES (~30-50 cP) that would occur at T-mixers. Since no reaction occurs at room temperature, premixing is safe and ensures homogeneous composition when nitrile oxide forms at high temperature.

### (3) Key Differences from Batch Protocol

- **Temperature elevation:** 140°C vs 120°C batch for accelerated kinetics
- **Pressure operation:** 5.0 bar BPR vs atmospheric batch
- **Residence time:** 2.8 min vs 15 min batch (5.4× reduction)
- **Heat transfer:** Narrow-bore tubing provides rapid, uniform heating vs oil bath gradients
- **Continuous operation:** Enables potential DES recycling and higher throughput

### (4) Engineering Validation Warnings

⚠️ **Mass transfer limitations identified:** Damköhler number (Da = 39.4) indicates potential mixing limitations in laminar flow, though acceptable for single-stream design.

⚠️ **Viscosity sensitivity:** DES viscosity temperature dependence may affect pressure drop during startup vs steady-state operation. Monitor pressure during heat-up.

⚠️ **Flow enhancement uncertainty:** The estimated 12.5× intensification factor from superheating is literature-based and requires experimental verification for this specific DES system.

**Recommended validation:** Verify residence time and conversion at design conditions before scale-up. Consider tracer studies to confirm mixing performance.

**Confidence: LOW** — The closest literature analogy has a similarity score of 0.38 (threshold for MEDIUM: 0.50). No close precedent found in the corpus for this exact reaction class and conditions. Treat this proposal as a starting hypothesis requiring careful experimental validation before scale-up.

case.title: Batch Synthesis of 3,5-Disubstituted Isoxazole in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
case.protocol: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).

chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism (well-established nitrile oxide [3+2] cycloaddition). The exact mechanism of nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate in DES (thermal dehydration vs. DES-mediated) is somewhat uncertain — literature suggests thermal elimination of water. Superheating to 140°C in flow is speculative but reasonable given the 15 min batch time at 120°C. DES viscosity at operating temperature is estimated; actual value should be measured for precise pressure drop and residence time calculations. The 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate accounts for competitive nitrile oxide dimerization; in flow with better mixing and temperature control, it may be possible to reduce to 1.5 equiv.

intensification_mandate.flow_justification_basis: Flow provides three key advantages: (1) Superheating under back-pressure regulation (e.g., 140°C, 5-10 bar BPR) accelerates nitrile oxide generation and cycloaddition, reducing residence time from 15 min to ~5 min. (2) Narrow-bore tubing (1.0 mm ID, stainless steel or PFA) provides excellent heat transfer (surface-to-volume ratio ~4000 m⁻¹) for rapid, uniform heating of the viscous DES, eliminating thermal gradients present in batch oil-bath heating. (3) Continuous operation with the DES enables potential solvent recycling and higher throughput without scale-up of the exothermic cycloaddition. Note: DES viscosity requires careful pressure drop calculation via Hagen-Poiseuille; a 1.0 mm ID × 2 m coil at 0.2 mL/min with ~20 cP viscosity (at 120°C) gives ΔP ≈ 3 bar, manageable with standard BPR.

analogies.summary: This photocycloaddition reaction utilizes triplet sensitization with 2,2'-dimethoxythioxanthone (2,2'-MeOTX) as the photocatalyst at 1 mol% loading under 455 nm COB LED irradiation. The flow process operates in acetonitrile at 200°C with a 5-minute residence time and 6.0 mL/min flow rate using a FlowSyn steel coil reactor (30 mL volume) equipped with a 34 bar back-pressure regulator. Flow chemistry enabled daisy-chaining of the initial photochemical step with high-temperature thermal cascade reactions, dramatically improving yield from 16% in batch (2 hours) to 88% in flow with a 24-fold intensification factor. The high-temperature operation required for the thermal cascade was much safer to implement in the continuous flow reactor compared to batch processing.

analogies.summary: This process involves the synthesis of 4-methyl-1,2,5-oxadiazole, a highly energetic compound, using a nitration or cyclization reaction in a concentrated sulfuric acid/water mixture (63:6.3 ratio). The flow process was conducted in a tubular reactor at 154°C with a residence time of 179 minutes (approximately 3 hours) and a flow rate of 0.12 mL/min, requiring a back-pressure regulator at 47.6 bar to maintain liquid phase conditions. Flow chemistry enabled a significant temperature increase from 65°C in batch to 154°C, resulting in an improved yield from 70.8% to 78.8%. The primary advantage of flow over batch was enhanced safety when handling this highly energetic compound, as the continuous process allows for better control of the exothermic reaction and reduces the inventory of hazardous material at any given time. The high pressure and temperature conditions achievable in the flow reactor also contributed to the improved reaction efficiency.

analogies.summary: This base-catalyzed nucleophilic ring-opening reaction was conducted in a cyclic continuous-flow reactor with 1.2 mm internal diameter tubing at 60°C using EtOAc as solvent. The flow process achieved 96% yield with a 60-minute residence time at 0.5 mL/min flow rate, compared to 93% yield requiring 20 hours in batch mode. The cyclic continuous-flow setup enabled a dramatic 20-fold reduction in reaction time (from 20 hours to 1 hour) while improving yield by 3 percentage points. The flow advantage likely stems from enhanced mass transfer and more consistent reaction conditions in the continuous system compared to the prolonged batch heating required for completion.

streams.pump_role: single premixed stream containing all reagents in DES
streams.reasoning: From Chemistry Plan stream_logic: Single premixed stream with both substrates dissolved in DES at room temperature before entering heated reactor. This ensures nitrile oxide intermediate is immediately trapped by dipolarophile upon formation, suppressing dimerization to furoxan.

proposal.mixing_order_reasoning: From Chemistry Plan: No mixer needed - single premixed stream. Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate are premixed in DES at room temperature where no reaction occurs, then pumped into heated coil reactor.
post_reactor_steps: Cool outlet stream to room temperature to stop reaction
proposal.chemistry_notes: Thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition driven by in-situ nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate at elevated temperature. Key design: premixed substrates in viscous DES (avoiding mixing issues) with superheating to 140°C under back-pressure for accelerated kinetics. Calculations show mass-transfer limitation (Da=39.4) but acceptable for single-stream design. BPR required for superheating above solvent boiling point.

reasoning_per_field.residence_time_min: Calculation estimated 0.8 min (range 0.5-2.2 min) based on 20× intensification factor. Analogy 1 used 5 min for photocycloaddition but different mechanism. Using calculation result as most reliable.
reasoning_per_field.flow_rate_mL_min: Calculation: 0.5 mL/min total flow rate to achieve 0.8 min residence time with 0.4 mL reactor volume. Analogy 3 used 0.5 mL/min for similar thermal process - good agreement.
reasoning_per_field.temperature_C: Chemistry Plan recommends 140°C superheating vs 120°C batch for accelerated kinetics. Analogy 1 used 200°C but for photochemistry. Analogy 2 used 154°C for thermal process - our 140°C is reasonable intermediate.
reasoning_per_field.concentration_M: Same as batch (0.5 M) - no dilution needed. Analogy 1 used 0.5 M successfully. Chemistry Plan indicates this concentration works well.
reasoning_per_field.BPR_bar: Calculation shows 5.0 bar required for superheating and gas solubility control. Essential for 140°C operation above solvent boiling point.
reasoning_per_field.reactor_type: Chemistry Plan specifies coil reactor for thermal process. All analogies used tubular/coil reactors.
reasoning_per_field.tubing_material: Stainless steel for high temperature (140°C) thermal process. No photochemistry so transparency not needed. Analogy 1 used steel successfully.
reasoning_per_field.tubing_ID_mm: Calculation used 1.0 mm ID giving acceptable pressure drop (0.002 bar) and laminar flow (Re=11). Analogy 3 used 1.2 mm - our 1.0 mm provides better heat transfer.
reasoning_per_field.reactor_volume_mL: Calculation: 0.38 mL rounded to 0.4 mL. Residence time = volume/flow rate = 0.4/0.5 = 0.8 min as required.
reasoning_per_field.light_setup: No light required - thermal reaction only. Chemistry Plan confirms no photochemistry involved.
reasoning_per_field.wavelength_nm: null - no photochemistry. Chemistry Plan explicitly states thermal mechanism only.
